title: Fix memory leak with exit files
body:
There's an excellent summary of the leak itself in the message of the first commit; suffice to say, we can't leave exit files around in tmpfs, it's causing a memory leak.

Instead, just use the events system to retrieve exit codes.

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Many thanks to Andrea Arcangeli for tracking the leak here down.
One test failing - `podman run --http-proxy`. Investigating.
It's an exit code with a `--rm` container, which is directly affected by this patch. Not clear why it's broken - works fine locally.
LGTM
Test failures look legitimate, will take a look later today

Problem: podman-in-podman tests. We're using events logging to retrieve the exit code - but there's no journald available for podman-in-podman, so no events. Evidently the result is that Podman times out?
I'm going to add a flag to manually specify events backend and force use of an events file
Updated. Tests should now all use the file backend for events.

`[+3876s] Error: error creating libpod runtime: error configuring CNI network plugin: could not create new watcher too many open files`

Eeek? I think we might be leaking FDs for events
Hm. I bet it's image caching/uncaching again... we're leaking a single lockfile per libpod runtime created.
I think we need to expose the /dev/null events backend and force it for uncaching/caching calls
Pushed a fresh version that exposes the null eventer and uses it for uncaching.
/retest
LGTM if you can chase down the test issues.
Lots of timeouts now. It looks like it's probably from reading events?

Alright, given the problems here, I'm probably going to split this into two PRs.

The first one will carry only the memory leak fix. The second will carry everything else, getting exit codes with `--rm` containers working again.

Hm. So we are definitely hitting the right code paths here - you can see it trying to get into events, it just can't find the `died` event.

I don't think this is a timing thing - if we're in this code, the container has already been removed, and remove should guarantee a Died event being recorded as part of it. Going to investigate further.
Hm. `podman events` is exiting with no output, but exit code 1.

Alright. There's a problem where at least some events are not being displayed (probably why events is throwing a nonzero exit code), but the core issue is that events only sees a single event - a refresh. Part of me wonders if we're seeing something happening that wipes the libpod directory before the last command is finished executing.

Finally green
@rhatdan @baude @TomSweeneyRedHat @vrothberg @giuseppe PTAL
Comments addressed - "null" renamed to "none" on the command line

LGTM
@baude @vrothberg @giuseppe @TomSweeneyRedHat @QiWang19 @ashley-cui PTAL
LGTM assuming happy tests
It's green, let's get this landed
/lgtm
